content:
AUDIENCE:
The reader is a senior business executive, likely in their 50s or 60s, with extensive leadership experience but no formal training in science or technology. They are highly results-oriented and time-pressed, valuing clear, concise explanations that connect technical ideas to practical business implications or real-world analogies. They are familiar with basic concepts like DNA from popular media but do not want complex jargon or deep scientific details. They care about understanding the "why" and "how" at a high level, especially if it relates to innovation, health, or the economy. Overly technical or lengthy explanations, or abstract scientific theory without context, would confuse or bore them. A confident, straightforward, and engaging tone that respects their intelligence and time will work best.

===BEGIN===
Inside every cell, messenger RNA (mRNA) acts like a set of instructions copied from our DNA, the cell’s master blueprint. Think of DNA as a company’s strategic plan stored safely in the office vault. When the cell needs to build a specific protein—its product or tool—it creates an mRNA copy of the relevant instructions. This mRNA then travels out to the cell’s “factory floor,” where proteins are made. In essence, mRNA delivers the precise recipe for making a protein, ensuring the cell produces exactly what it needs, when it needs it. This process is crucial for everything from growth to responding to changes. Understanding mRNA’s role has opened doors to innovations like mRNA vaccines, which teach cells to make proteins that help protect against diseases—showing how biology’s instruction manual can translate into real-world impact.
===END===
